vocabulary.name: Abstract API Vocabulary
vocabulary.description: Domain vocabulary for the Abstract design version control and collaboration API.

terms.definition: The top-level entity in Abstract that groups projects and manages team members. An organization has a unique ID, a name, and may have logo, domain restrictions, and subscription settings.

A design project that belongs to an organization. Projects contain branches, files, and a full version history of design changes. Projects can be cloud-based or local and have visibility settings (organization-wide or specific teams).

A parallel line of design work within a project, analogous to a git branch. Branches allow designers to explore changes independently before merging them. Every project has a 'master' branch representing the source of truth.
A saved snapshot of changes pushed to a branch. Each commit has a SHA identifier, a title, a description, and references to which files changed. Commits form the version history of a branch.
A unique hash identifier for a specific commit, used to reference a precise point in the version history of a branch or file. The value 'latest' refers to the most recent commit.
A design file (typically a Sketch .sketch file) stored within a branch at a specific commit SHA. Files can be design files or shared libraries.
A page within a design file. A file can have multiple pages, each containing design layers arranged in a canvas.
An individual design element within a page — such as a group, shape, text, symbol, or artboard. Layers can be inspected for design properties and exported as assets.
A curated set of layers from across files and branches, assembled for review, handoff, or presentation purposes. Collections are published to share design intent with stakeholders.
A reference to a specific layer added to a collection, tracking the file, page, layer, and commit SHA of the layer. Collection layers can be pinned or set to track the latest commit.
An exported asset generated from a design layer, such as a PNG, SVG, or PDF export. Assets are associated with a layer, file, and project and can be downloaded as binary data.

A threaded comment that can be attached to a branch, commit, page, or specific layer position (via an annotation). Comments support design review workflows and can carry review status (REQUESTED, APPROVED, REJECTED).
A positional marker on a design layer used to attach a comment to a specific area. Annotations include x/y coordinates, width, height, and scale relative to the layer.
A structured diff between two states of a branch or between a commit and its parent, showing which files, pages, layers, colors, text styles, and other elements were added, edited, or deleted.
The status of an individual change within a changeset. Possible values: 'added', 'deleted', 'edited', 'edited-indirectly', 'none'.

The merge readiness state of a branch relative to its parent. Possible values: 'CLEAN' (ready to merge), 'NEEDS_UPDATE' (needs changes from parent pulled in), 'NEEDS_REMOTE_UPDATE' (needs remote sync).
The status of a design review on a branch comment. Possible values: 'REQUESTED' (review asked for), 'APPROVED' (reviewer approved), 'REJECTED' (reviewer requested changes).
A user's association with an organization or project, including their role (guest, member, owner) and subscription role (viewer, contributor) within that context.
A grouping mechanism for organizing projects within an organization. Sections can be starred for quick access.
A bookmark created by a user to mark a project or section as a favorite for quick access.
A record of a significant event within a project or organization, such as branch creation, commits, merges, comments, or review requests. Activities form an audit trail and feed notification streams.
A user-facing alert generated from an activity, delivered within the Abstract platform to inform team members about events relevant to them.
A subscription that delivers HTTP POST payloads to an external URL when specific Abstract events occur. Webhooks are configured at the organization level and can filter for specific event types.
A record of a single attempt by Abstract to deliver a webhook payload to the configured endpoint, including the request/response headers, body, and HTTP status code.
A shareable link to an Abstract resource (project, branch, file, page, layer, comment, or collection) that can be distributed to stakeholders who may or may not have an Abstract account.
A rendered image preview of a specific layer version, available as a URL or as raw binary data (e.g. PNG). Previews allow visual inspection of design states without the native application.
The full structured design data for a layer version, including all visual properties such as fills, borders, shadows, typography, and nested layer structure.

An OAuth access token generated through the Abstract web application, used to authenticate API requests. Tokens can be provided via the ABSTRACT_TOKEN environment variable or passed directly when initializing the SDK client.
The mechanism through which the Abstract SDK communicates with the platform — either the HTTP API transport (requests to api.goabstract.com) or the CLI transport (local command-line interface).
A pagination mechanism for list endpoints that return large result sets. A cursor represents a position in the result set and is used to fetch the next page of results via offset-based pagination.
A lightweight object that identifies a specific Abstract resource by its IDs. Descriptors are passed to SDK endpoint methods to specify which resource to retrieve or operate on.
